name: Angkor Wat
uniqueFact: The largest religious complex in the world, covering 162.6 hectares. It is the only temple in the Angkor region oriented to the west, symbolizing the direction of death and the setting sun — reflecting its original role as a funerary temple.
highlights: Iconic five-tower silhouette reflected in the surrounding moat
Over 1,700 apsara (celestial dancer) carvings on the walls
Famous sunrise viewpoint drawing thousands of visitors daily
Upper sanctuary (Bakan) offers panoramic views — opens 7:40 AM
tip: Arrive before 5:30 AM for the iconic sunrise reflection over the moat.

name: Ta Prohm
uniqueFact: Known as the 'Jungle Temple' and the 'Tomb Raider Temple', Ta Prohm has been intentionally left unrestored, with massive silk-cotton and strangler fig trees growing directly through its towers and galleries — a rare and haunting sight.
highlights: Giant tree roots engulfing ancient stone walls and doorways
Featured filming location for the movie Tomb Raider (2001)
Serene and atmospheric, especially beautiful at dawn
Sanskrit stone inscription details 3,140 villages that once supported the temple
tip: Visit before 10:00 AM — the temple becomes very crowded and noisy by late morning.

name: Angkor Thom
uniqueFact: Angkor Thom ('Great City') was the last capital of the Khmer Empire and once home to nearly one million people — making it one of the largest pre-industrial cities in the world. The city is enclosed by an 8-meter-high wall and a wide moat.
highlights: Five massive entrance gates each topped with giant four-faced towers
Contains Bayon Temple at its center, famous for 216 serene stone faces
Terrace of the Elephants — a 350-meter royal viewing platform
Terrace of the Leper King with intricate multi-layered bas-relief carvings
tip: Allow at least half a day — Angkor Thom contains multiple major sites within its walls.

name: Bayon Temple
khmerName: ប្រាសាទបាយ័ន
uniqueFact: Bayon's 54 towers each feature four massive faces gazing in the cardinal directions — a total of 216 serene faces that are believed to represent either the Bodhisattva Avalokitesvara or King Jayavarman VII himself watching over his kingdom.
highlights: 216 giant stone faces watching from every angle
Over 1.2 km of bas-relief galleries depicting everyday Khmer life and battles
Atmospheric at midday when the sun illuminates the faces
Located at the very center of Angkor Thom
tip: Look for the fascinating bas-reliefs on the lower level showing markets, fishing, and cockfighting — a rare glimpse of everyday Khmer life.

name: Phnom Bakheng
uniqueFact: Built on a natural 67-meter hill, Phnom Bakheng was the first temple-mountain at Angkor and predates Angkor Wat by over 200 years. It was the state temple of the first Angkorian capital, Yasodharapura.
highlights: Best sunset viewpoint in the Angkor region — views of Angkor Wat in the distance
108 surrounding towers symbolizing the 4 lunar phases
Located between Angkor Wat and Angkor Thom — easy to combine
One of three hilltop temples built by Yasovarman I
tip: Arrive at least 45 minutes before sunset — the viewpoint has a visitor cap and fills up quickly.

name: Banteay Srei
khmerName: បន្ទាយស្រី
uniqueFact: Often called the 'Jewel of Khmer Art', Banteay Srei is built almost entirely of pink sandstone and features the most intricate and well-preserved decorative carvings in all of Angkor. Unlike most Angkorian temples, it was not built by a king but by a court official.
highlights: Extraordinary pink sandstone carvings considered the finest in the Khmer world
Unusually small and intimate scale compared to other Angkor temples
Dedicated to Shiva — devata (female deity) carvings are exceptionally detailed
Consecrated April 22, 967 AD — one of the few Angkorian temples with a known exact date
tip: Combine with Beng Mealea on the same day trip as they are both northeast of Siem Reap.

name: Phnom Krom

location.region: Southern Siem Reap, Tonle Sap shoreline

uniqueFact: Perched on a 137-meter hill overlooking the Tonle Sap Lake, Phnom Krom is one of three hilltop temples built by Yasovarman I. Its location offers dramatic panoramic views of the vast lake and surrounding floodplains — especially spectacular at sunrise.
highlights: Three towers dedicated to the Hindu trinity: Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva
Stunning sunrise and lake views over the Tonle Sap — Southeast Asia's largest lake
Far fewer tourists than the main Angkor complex
Active Buddhist monastery at the base of the hill
tip: Visit at sunrise for breathtaking views over the Tonle Sap. Can be combined with a boat trip on the lake.

name: Temple of Preah Vihear
style: Khmer / Baphuon and Angkor Wat styles
uniqueFact: Dramatically perched on a 525-meter cliff on the Cambodian-Thai border, Preah Vihear was the subject of a landmark 1962 International Court of Justice ruling awarding it to Cambodia. The temple stretches 800 meters along a north-south axis rising up to the sanctuary at the clifftop.
highlights: Breathtaking clifftop location with views across the Cambodian plains
Five ceremonial gopuras (gateways) rising up the hillside
UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2008
Ancient sandstone stairway dating to the 11th century still in use
tip: A full-day trip from Siem Reap. Hire a local guide — the history and border dispute context make the visit far richer.

name: Koh Ker
uniqueFact: Koh Ker served as the capital of the Khmer Empire for 20 years under King Jayavarman IV. Its centerpiece, Prasat Thom, is a seven-tiered pyramid standing 36 meters tall — the tallest Khmer pyramid ever built. The site encompasses over 180 temple structures across the jungle.
highlights: Prasat Thom — a 36-meter seven-tiered sandstone pyramid climbable to the top
Remote jungle setting with many temples still partially reclaimed by trees
UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023
Over 180 structures spread across the area, many yet to be fully explored
tip: Often combined with Beng Mealea on a full-day trip from Siem Reap. The road has greatly improved in recent years.

name: Beng Mealea
uniqueFact: Built in the same style and era as Angkor Wat but left almost entirely unrestored, Beng Mealea is dramatically consumed by jungle vegetation with massive stones collapsed into great heaps. Wooden walkways guide visitors through and above the ruins for a true 'lost city' experience.
highlights: Vast ruins almost entirely consumed by jungle — rarely crowded
Wooden elevated walkways provide safe and dramatic access through the collapse
Same architectural style as Angkor Wat — like seeing it before restoration
Diverse birdlife and tranquil jungle atmosphere
tip: An excellent half-day trip often combined with Koh Ker. Go early for the best light and fewest crowds.

name: Prasat Wat (Wat Athvea)
khmerName: ប្រាសាទវត្តអ័ថ្វា
religion: Hindu (Vishnu), with active Buddhist pagoda alongside
uniqueFact: Wat Athvea is unique in that an ancient Vishnuite Khmer temple and a living modern Buddhist pagoda share the same grounds — making it both an archaeological site and an active place of worship. Its name means 'the way' or 'the pass' in Khmer.
highlights: Rare combination of an ancient Hindu temple alongside a functioning Buddhist temple
Exceptionally detailed devata carvings in the style of Angkor Wat
Very few tourists — a peaceful alternative to the main circuit
Located just off the road to the Tonle Sap — easy to combine with a lake visit
tip: A hidden gem close to Siem Reap. Respectful dress required as it is an active religious site.

name: Preah Khan
uniqueFact: Built on the site of the battle where Jayavarman VII defeated the Cham invaders in 1191, Preah Khan ('Sacred Sword') served as both a royal city and a major monastic university with over 1,000 teachers and nearly 100,000 people supporting it.
highlights: Long atmospheric jungle corridors with dappled light filtering through the trees
Unique two-story circular columned structure unlike anything else at Angkor
Much less crowded than Angkor Wat or Ta Prohm
Contains a rare depiction of a two-story building in Angkorian architecture
tip: One of the Grand Circuit's highlights — quieter and more exploratory than the small circuit temples.
